name: The Object Particle o
summary: The particle を (o) marks the direct object - the thing an action is done to. It sits right before the verb's noun.
explanation:
When an action affects a thing, that thing is the direct object, and Japanese marks it with the particle を. Although the kana を was historically 'wo', as a particle it is pronounced simply 'o'. The pattern is [object] を [verb]: パンを食べます (pan o tabemasu) means 'I eat bread', where パン (bread) is what is eaten.

Because Japanese is subject-object-verb, the を-marked object always comes before the verb. You can add a topic with は at the front: 私はパンを食べます (watashi wa pan o tabemasu), 'As for me, I eat bread'. The を simply tells the listener which noun the verb is acting on.

を is also used with motion verbs to mark the space passed through, as in 公園を歩きます (kouen o arukimasu), 'I walk through the park'. For now, focus on the core use: marking the thing that receives the action.

examples.native: 水を飲みます。(mizu o nomimasu.)
本を読みます。(hon o yomimasu.)
映画を見ました。(eiga o mimashita.)
手紙を書きます。(tegami o kakimasu.)
音楽を聞きます。(ongaku o kikimasu.)
ドアを開けてください。(doa o akete kudasai.)

commonMistakes: Pronouncing を as 'wo'. As a particle it is read 'o', the same as お.
Using が instead of を for the object. The thing acted upon takes を, not が.
Placing the verb before the object. The を-marked object must come before the verb.
Adding を to verbs that do not take a direct object, such as 行く (iku, to go), which uses に or へ.
Forgetting that some verbs of motion use を for the path travelled, not the object.
tip: Ask 'what is being done to?' The answer takes を and sits just before the verb.
